walkthrough.title: Unbox the Central Board
walkthrough.text: Let's get Libertalia set up. First, find the central island board and place it in the middle of the table where everyone can reach it. This board has spaces for loot tokens across the top and a reputation track along one side. It is the heart of the game where all the action happens.

walkthrough.title: Set Up the Reputation Track
walkthrough.text: Each player takes a reputation token in their chosen color and places it on the middle space of the reputation track on the island board. Reputation is used to break ties when two players play the same character card. Everyone starts at the same level.

walkthrough.title: Prepare the Score Track
walkthrough.text: Place the score track where everyone can see it and put each player's score marker on zero. This track measures doubloons, which is how you win the game. You will move up this track throughout all three voyages as you collect loot and trigger abilities.

walkthrough.title: Sort the Character Cards
walkthrough.text: Now let's deal with the character cards. Each player gets an identical set of forty character cards numbered one through forty. Every card has a unique pirate crew member with special abilities. Make sure each player has a complete set sorted by number so it is easy to find specific cards later.

walkthrough.title: Deal the Starting Hand
walkthrough.text: Randomly draw six character card numbers. You can do this by having one player shuffle their deck and draw six cards, then announce the numbers. Every other player finds those same six cards in their own deck. All players now hold the exact same hand of six characters. Set aside the rest of your deck for later voyages.

walkthrough.title: Prepare the Loot Bag
walkthrough.text: Find all the loot tokens and place them in the cloth loot bag. Give the bag a good shake to mix them up. These tokens represent the treasure your crew will be fighting over each day. There are many different types with different effects, so you will learn them as they appear.

walkthrough.title: Draw Loot for the First Voyage
walkthrough.text: Each voyage lasts four days at two or three players, five days at four or five players, and six days at six players. For each day of the voyage, draw loot tokens from the bag equal to the number of players and place them face up in a row on the island board. So if you have four players, you will draw five rows of four tokens each.

walkthrough.title: Hand Out Player Boards
walkthrough.text: Give each player a player board. This is your personal ship where you will organize your played characters and collected loot. It has a tableau area where your characters go after they are played, and a graveyard area where characters go at the end of each voyage.

walkthrough.title: Set Starting Doubloons
walkthrough.text: Each player begins the game with zero doubloons on the score track. Some character abilities and loot tokens will grant or deduct doubloons throughout the game. Make sure everyone's score marker is clearly on the zero space.

walkthrough.title: Choose the First Player
walkthrough.text: Pick a starting player at random and give them the Midshipman token. This token indicates who goes first when there are ties or when order matters. The Midshipman token will change hands throughout the game based on certain character abilities.

walkthrough.title: Review the Loot Display
walkthrough.text: Before you start playing, take a moment with your group to look at the loot tokens laid out on the island board. The top row is day one, the next row is day two, and so on. You can see what loot is available for every day of the voyage, which is critical for planning which characters to play and when.

walkthrough.title: Ready to Sail
walkthrough.text: Setup is complete. Each player should have six character cards in hand, a player board, and a score marker on zero. The island board should have loot tokens laid out for the entire first voyage. The reputation tokens should all be in the center of the reputation track. Let's learn how to play.

walkthrough.title: Game Overview
walkthrough.text: Libertalia is played over three voyages. Each voyage consists of several days where your pirate crew competes for loot. The twist is that every player starts each voyage with the exact same characters, so you must outguess your opponents about which crew member to play and when.

walkthrough.title: The Structure of a Day
walkthrough.text: Each day within a voyage follows four phases in order: Sunrise, Daytime, Dusk, and Night. Everyone participates in each phase before moving to the next. After all four phases, played characters go to your ship tableau, and the voyage advances to the next day.

walkthrough.title: Sunrise: Choose Your Character
walkthrough.text: At Sunrise, all players simultaneously choose one character card from their hand and place it face down on the table. Once everyone is ready, all cards are revealed at the same time. This is the core decision of the game. You are trying to predict what everyone else will play while picking the best character for the available loot.

walkthrough.title: Daytime: Resolve Day Abilities
walkthrough.text: During the Daytime phase, characters with a sun icon ability resolve their powers from lowest rank to highest rank. Low-numbered characters act first in this phase. Daytime abilities are often aggressive, letting you steal loot, discard opponents' characters, or manipulate the board before loot is collected.

walkthrough.title: Dusk: Collect Loot
walkthrough.text: Now we flip the order. During Dusk, the highest-ranked character picks first. Going from highest to lowest rank, each player selects one loot token from that day's row on the island board. Higher-ranked characters get first pick of the best loot, while lower-ranked characters are stuck with whatever remains.

walkthrough.title: Night: Resolve Night Abilities
walkthrough.text: After loot collection, characters with a moon icon ability resolve their Night powers. These often trigger based on what loot you collected or the state of the board. Night abilities resolve from lowest rank to highest, just like Daytime.

walkthrough.title: Rest: Characters Go to Your Ship
walkthrough.text: After the Night phase, all played character cards move to their owner's ship tableau on the player board. Characters on your ship may have ongoing effects or anchor abilities that trigger at the end of the voyage. The next day begins with a new Sunrise phase.

walkthrough.title: Tie-Breaking with Reputation
walkthrough.text: When two or more players play the same character, they have the same rank number. Ties are broken using the reputation track. The player with higher reputation resolves first during Daytime, picks loot first during Dusk, and resolves Night abilities first. Managing your reputation gives you a consistent advantage in close matchups.

walkthrough.title: Understanding Ability Timing
walkthrough.text: Each character card can have abilities that trigger at different times, marked by icons. The sun icon means Daytime, which resolves low to high. The sunset icon means Dusk, which triggers when collecting loot. The moon icon means Night, resolving low to high. The anchor icon means end of voyage. Some characters also have graveyard effects that trigger when discarded.

walkthrough.title: Loot Token Types
walkthrough.text: Loot tokens come in many varieties. Treasure chests are worth doubloons at voyage end. Maps score based on set collection, getting more valuable the more you collect. Cursed relics cost you doubloons, but some characters actually benefit from them. Sabers provide combat bonuses. Other special loot tokens let you steal, swap, or gain unique advantages.

walkthrough.title: End of a Voyage
walkthrough.text: When all days in a voyage are complete, the voyage ends and several things happen in order. First, resolve all anchor abilities on characters sitting in your ship tableau. Second, resolve all loot token effects, which may grant or deduct doubloons. Third, discard all loot tokens. Fourth, move all characters from your ship to your graveyard.

walkthrough.title: Between Voyages: Growing Hands
walkthrough.text: Before Voyage two begins, deal six new character card numbers the same way you did at the start. All players find those same cards in their remaining deck. The key twist: you keep any leftover cards from the previous voyage. So your hand grows from six cards in Voyage one to potentially eight or more by Voyage three.

walkthrough.title: Card Memory and Deduction
walkthrough.text: Since all players receive the same characters, you can track what opponents have played. If you saw someone play the Brute on day two, you know they no longer have it. This deduction element is central to the game. Pay attention to what characters hit the table and work out what is still in opponents' hands.

walkthrough.title: The Graveyard
walkthrough.text: Characters that leave your ship go to your graveyard, which is visible to all players. Some character abilities reference the graveyard, either gaining power from characters already there or interacting with opponents' graveyards. Keep your graveyard organized so everyone can see what you have discarded.

walkthrough.title: Loot Setup Between Voyages
walkthrough.text: At the start of each new voyage, draw fresh loot tokens from the bag for every day of the upcoming voyage. Just like the first voyage, all loot is placed face up so everyone can see the entire voyage's loot before playing a single card. This transparency is what makes the planning and bluffing so engaging.

walkthrough.title: Scoring and Endgame
walkthrough.text: After the third voyage ends and all anchor and loot effects are resolved, the game is over. Players total their doubloons from the score track. The player with the most doubloons wins. If there is a tie, the player with higher reputation wins the tiebreaker.

walkthrough.title: Welcome to Your First Voyage
walkthrough.text: Welcome to your first voyage of Libertalia! In this practice tutorial, we will walk through a scripted first voyage with four example players. Each player will make different choices so you can see how the simultaneous selection and ability timing works. Follow along even if you have fewer players at your table.

walkthrough.title: The Setup
walkthrough.text: Everyone has the same six character cards in hand. The island board shows loot tokens for each day of the voyage. Before playing anything, let's scan the loot. Day one has two treasure chests and two cursed relics. Day two has a map and three treasure chests. Knowing the loot for every day is how you plan your strategy.

walkthrough.title: Day One: Sunrise
walkthrough.text: It is Sunrise on day one. Everyone picks a character card simultaneously. Player one plays a high-ranked character, hoping to grab a treasure chest. Player two plays a low-ranked character with a strong Daytime ability. Player three plays a mid-ranked character. Player four also plays the same high-ranked character as Player one. Uh oh, a collision!

walkthrough.title: Day One: Resolving the Collision
walkthrough.text: When two players play the same character, reputation breaks the tie. Player one has higher reputation than Player four, so Player one resolves first in every phase today. This is why reputation matters. Player four will pick loot after Player one, potentially getting stuck with a cursed relic instead of a treasure chest.

walkthrough.title: Day One: Daytime Phase
walkthrough.text: Now we resolve Daytime abilities from lowest rank to highest. Player two's low-ranked character goes first and uses its Daytime ability to steal a doubloon from Player three. Low-ranked characters act first during the day, which is their big advantage. The higher-ranked characters resolve their Daytime abilities after, but in this case they have no sun abilities.

walkthrough.title: Day One: Dusk Phase
walkthrough.text: At Dusk, we flip the order. Highest rank picks loot first. Player one picks a treasure chest since they have higher reputation than Player four. Player four, with the same rank but lower reputation, picks next and grabs the other treasure chest. Player three picks next, and Player two, with the lowest rank, is stuck with a cursed relic. Low rank gets first Daytime ability but last loot pick. That is the core trade-off.

walkthrough.title: The Core Trade-Off
walkthrough.text: Let's pause and highlight this. Low-ranked characters act first during Daytime but pick loot last at Dusk. High-ranked characters pick loot first but their Daytime abilities resolve last. This tension is the heart of every decision in Libertalia. You are constantly weighing whether you need a strong early ability or first pick of loot.

walkthrough.title: Player Four's Mistake: Not Reading the Loot
walkthrough.text: Let's look at what Player four did wrong. They played the same high-ranked character as Player one without checking reputation. Since Player four had lower reputation, they did not get first pick between the two. Before playing a contested character, always check if someone else might play the same card and whether your reputation gives you the edge.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Check Reputation Before Mirroring
walkthrough.text: The takeaway is simple. Since everyone has the same cards, collisions are inevitable. When you suspect another player might play the same character, check the reputation track. If your reputation is lower, consider playing a different character where you will not face a tiebreaker disadvantage. Avoiding collisions entirely is often the best strategy.

walkthrough.title: Day Two: Reading the Loot
walkthrough.text: Before day two's Sunrise, look at the loot row. There is a map and three treasure chests. Maps get more valuable the more you collect, so Player two really wants that map. Player two plays a high-ranked character to guarantee first pick. Player one plays a character with a useful Night ability. Player three plays a mid-ranked character. Player four plays a low-ranked character with a strong Daytime ability.

walkthrough.title: Day Two: Abilities and Loot
walkthrough.text: Daytime resolves low to high. Player four's low character fires first, gaining two reputation from their ability. At Dusk, Player two picks first with the highest rank and grabs the map. Everyone else takes treasure chests. During Night, Player one's character activates, gaining a bonus doubloon for having a treasure chest. Notice how Player one planned their character choice around the Night ability triggering with the loot they expected to collect.

walkthrough.title: Player Four's Mistake: Wasting a Powerful Card
walkthrough.text: Player four played a character with a strong Daytime ability on a day where the loot was all positive. There was nothing to disrupt and no cursed relics to force on opponents. The Daytime ability was essentially wasted. Meanwhile, day three has cursed relics mixed in with treasure, which would have been a much better day for a disruptive low-ranked character.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Match Characters to the Day's Loot
walkthrough.text: Always read the loot before choosing your character. Disruptive Daytime abilities are most valuable when the loot display includes cursed relics or high-value items worth fighting over. Save your aggressive characters for those contested days. On days where all the loot is similar, a high-ranked character for first pick is usually better than a disruptive low-ranked one.

walkthrough.title: Day Three: The Anchor Ability
walkthrough.text: On day three, Player one plays a character with an anchor icon. This ability will not trigger today. Instead, it fires at the end of the entire voyage when anchor abilities resolve. Player one is thinking ahead. That anchor ability awards bonus doubloons based on how many treasure chests you collected during the voyage.

walkthrough.title: Planning Across the Voyage
walkthrough.text: This is where Libertalia gets deep. You are not just playing one day at a time. You need to plan which characters to save for which days based on the entire loot display. Anchor abilities reward you for what you accumulate across the whole voyage. Night abilities should pair with the loot you expect to collect that day. Think at least two days ahead when choosing your character.

walkthrough.title: Player Four's Mistake: Ignoring Leftover Cards
walkthrough.text: By day four, Player four has used all their strongest characters and is left with only weak options. Meanwhile, Player two saved a versatile mid-ranked character for the last day, when there is less competition and the loot includes a valuable map. Player four burned through their best cards too early without considering the full voyage.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Pace Your Hand
walkthrough.text: Do not play your best characters on days one and two just because you are excited. Look at the loot for every day of the voyage and decide which day each character will be most impactful. Sometimes your best play is saving a strong character for day five when the loot is incredible, even if it means taking mediocre loot on day one.

walkthrough.title: End of Voyage Resolution
walkthrough.text: All days are done. Now we resolve the end of the voyage. First, anchor abilities fire for characters on each player's ship. Player one gains bonus doubloons from their treasure chest collection. Next, loot token effects resolve. Treasure chests add doubloons, maps score based on collection size, and cursed relics deduct doubloons. Then all loot is discarded and ship characters move to the graveyard.

walkthrough.title: Preparing for Voyage Two
walkthrough.text: Before Voyage two starts, deal six new character numbers. All players find those same cards in their remaining decks. But remember, you keep leftover cards from Voyage one. If you did not use all six cards in Voyage one, those extra cards carry into Voyage two, giving you more options than your opponents who used everything.

walkthrough.title: Congratulations
walkthrough.text: Congratulations on completing your first voyage of Libertalia! You have seen how simultaneous selection works, how reputation breaks ties, how to read the loot display, and how to plan across multiple days. You watched Player four learn from three common mistakes: not checking reputation before mirroring, wasting disruptive characters on the wrong day, and burning through strong cards too early.

walkthrough.title: Before You Set Sail
walkthrough.text:
Before you dive into a full game, here are three things that can help.

First, check out General Tips for strategy basics and common mistakes to avoid.

Second, reference the Appendix for a quick guide to ability timing, loot types, and game terms.

And most importantly, ask your personal GM Guide for help anytime. Just tap the Q&A tab and ask any question during your game. Have fun!

summary.title: Before You Set Sail
bullets: Check out General Tips → strategy basics for beginners
Reference the Appendix → quick-reference for abilities and terms
Ask your personal GM Guide → tap Q&A for instant answers during play

walkthrough.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
walkthrough.text: Before we get into strategy, here are the most common mistakes new Libertalia players make. First, playing characters without looking at the loot display. The loot should drive every decision. Second, always playing your highest-ranked character first, which burns your best cards early. Third, ignoring the reputation track, which can cost you tiebreakers at the worst possible moment. Fourth, forgetting that everyone has the same cards and failing to anticipate what opponents will play.

walkthrough.title: Read the Loot Before Every Day
walkthrough.text: This cannot be overstated. Before choosing your character at Sunrise, study the loot tokens for the current day and all remaining days of the voyage. If today's loot is mediocre but tomorrow has two treasure chests, consider playing a weaker character today and saving your powerhouse for tomorrow. The loot display is perfect information, and using it is the biggest edge you can get.

walkthrough.title: Think About What Opponents Will Play
walkthrough.text: The simultaneous selection is what makes Libertalia shine. Before placing your card face down, think about what your opponents are likely to play. If there is one amazing loot token, everyone will be tempted to play a high-ranked character. Maybe you can surprise them with a low-ranked character that has a powerful Daytime ability instead. The best plays in Libertalia are the ones that account for what opponents are thinking.

walkthrough.title: Manage Your Reputation
walkthrough.text: Reputation is not just a tiebreaker. It is a strategic resource that gives you priority whenever a collision happens. If two players play the same character, higher reputation picks loot first and resolves abilities first. Actively pursue reputation-boosting abilities and loot throughout the game. Even a single step ahead on the reputation track can swing multiple loot picks in your favor across a voyage.

walkthrough.title: Pace Your Cards Across the Voyage
walkthrough.text: With six cards and five or six days, you may not play every card. This means you get to choose which days to deploy your strongest crew members. Resist the temptation to play all your best characters on days one and two. Instead, map out which character is most impactful on which day based on the loot available. Sometimes the best play is accepting bad loot on day one so you can dominate days three and four.

walkthrough.title: Cursed Relics Are Not Always Bad
walkthrough.text: New players instinctively avoid cursed relics, but several characters actually benefit from having them. Some Daytime abilities let you force cursed relics onto opponents, while other Night abilities convert your own relics into advantages. Before automatically avoiding cursed loot, check whether any characters in your hand can turn it into a weapon or a benefit.

walkthrough.title: Anchor Abilities Are Secret Weapons
walkthrough.text: Characters with anchor abilities reward you for your entire voyage's performance, not just a single day. A character that grants bonus doubloons for each treasure chest you collected is worth more when played on an early day where you collect the chest, then sit on your ship accumulating value for the rest of the voyage. Play anchor characters early so they have time to maximize their end-of-voyage payoff.

walkthrough.title: Track What Opponents Have Played
walkthrough.text: Since every player starts with the same hand, you have perfect information about what characters exist. As each day passes and cards are revealed, you can narrow down what remains in opponents' hands. By day four of a voyage, you may know exactly what each opponent is holding. Use this information to avoid collisions when your reputation is low or to force collisions when your reputation is high.

summary.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
bullets: Don't pick characters without reading the loot display
Don't always lead with your highest-ranked card
Don't ignore the reputation track — it decides tiebreakers
Don't forget opponents have the same cards — anticipate their plays

summary.title: Read the Loot Before Every Day
bullets: Study loot for current day AND all remaining days
Save strong characters for high-value loot days
Loot display is perfect information — use it fully

summary.title: Think About What Opponents Will Play
bullets: If amazing loot exists, everyone may play high-rank
Counter by going low with a strong Daytime ability
Best plays account for opponents' likely choices

summary.title: Manage Your Reputation
bullets: Reputation = priority in every tiebreaker situation
Actively pursue reputation-boosting abilities and loot
One step ahead on rep track swings multiple loot picks per voyage

summary.title: Pace Your Cards Across the Voyage
bullets: You may not play every card — choose deployments carefully
Map characters to the day they'll have most impact
Accept bad loot early to dominate high-value days later

summary.title: Cursed Relics Are Not Always Bad
bullets: Some characters convert cursed relics into advantages
Daytime abilities can force relics onto opponents
Check your hand for relic synergies before avoiding them

summary.title: Anchor Abilities Are Secret Weapons
bullets: Anchor abilities reward entire voyage performance
Play anchor characters early to maximize end-of-voyage payoff
Pair with loot collection strategy for big bonus doubloons

summary.title: Track What Opponents Have Played
bullets: All players start with same hand → track plays to deduce hands
By day 4, you may know opponents' exact remaining cards
Force collisions when your rep is high, avoid them when low

walkthrough.title: The Loot Specialist Strategy
walkthrough.text: One of the strongest approaches in Libertalia is committing to a loot type across the entire game. Maps reward set collection, becoming exponentially more valuable the more you collect. If you can grab three or four maps across three voyages, the doubloon payoff is enormous. Build your character selection around guaranteed map picks by playing high-ranked characters on map days and saving disruptive characters for days when opponents threaten your collection.

walkthrough.title: The Reputation Engine
walkthrough.text: Reputation is not just a tiebreaker. It is a compounding advantage. If you invest early in climbing the reputation track, every collision for the rest of the game goes in your favor. This means you can confidently play the same character as an opponent, knowing you will pick loot first. Characters with reputation-boosting abilities become much stronger when played early in a voyage. Prioritize reputation in Voyage one to reap benefits in Voyages two and three.

walkthrough.title: The Disruption Strategy
walkthrough.text: Instead of chasing the best loot yourself, focus on denying it to others. Low-ranked characters with aggressive Daytime abilities let you steal loot, discard opponents' characters, or move cursed relics onto their boards. This strategy works best when one opponent is pulling ahead. Force them into bad loot picks and collisions while you accumulate steady, modest gains. The disruption player rarely wins by a lot, but they often win consistently.

walkthrough.title: Reading and Exploiting Collisions
walkthrough.text: Advanced players actively seek or avoid collisions based on position. If you have the highest reputation and suspect an opponent will play a specific character, mirroring them guarantees you resolve first. Conversely, if your reputation is low, never play a character you think someone else will choose. Track the cards played each day and calculate what remains in opponents' hands. By the last two days of a voyage, you can often predict opponents' plays with near certainty.

walkthrough.title: Voyage Pacing and Card Conservation
walkthrough.text: Leftover cards from one voyage carry into the next. In Voyage one, if you play only four of your six cards, you enter Voyage two with eight cards while opponents who played all six have only six. Those extra cards give you more flexibility and make it harder for opponents to predict your plays. Consider deliberately holding back one or two cards in early voyages to build a larger hand advantage later.

walkthrough.title: Anchor Timing Optimization
walkthrough.text: Characters with anchor abilities should be played strategically within the voyage. Playing an anchor character early means it sits on your ship for more days, but it also means you used that character slot on a potentially weak loot day. The optimal play is to use anchor characters on days when the loot itself is mediocre, so you do not waste a strong character on a low-value day. The anchor ability provides the value instead of the loot.

walkthrough.title: Graveyard Synergies Across Voyages
walkthrough.text: Some characters gain power based on what is in your graveyard. Since characters move from ship to graveyard at the end of each voyage, your graveyard grows as the game progresses. By Voyage three, you may have eight or more characters in your graveyard. Characters that score based on graveyard count or specific graveyard contents become devastatingly strong in the late game. Plan your Voyage one and two plays partially around setting up your graveyard for Voyage three payoffs.

walkthrough.title: Multiplayer Threat Assessment
walkthrough.text: In games with four or more players, paying attention to the score track is critical. Identify who is leading after each voyage and adjust your strategy accordingly. If the leader is pursuing maps, play characters that steal or discard loot on map-heavy days. If they have high reputation, target them with reputation-lowering abilities. The best advanced players do not just play their own game. They monitor the table and make strategic plays that keep the leader in check while advancing their own position.

summary.title: The Loot Specialist Strategy
bullets: Commit to one loot type (especially maps) across all 3 voyages
Maps scale exponentially — 3-4 maps = huge doubloon payoff
Play high-rank on map days, save disruptors for threats to collection

summary.title: The Reputation Engine
bullets: Invest in reputation early — it compounds across the whole game
High rep lets you mirror opponents and win every tiebreaker
Reputation-boosting characters are strongest when played early in V1

summary.title: The Disruption Strategy
bullets: Focus on denying opponents loot rather than chasing the best loot
Low-rank Daytime abilities: steal, discard, force cursed relics
Target the leader — steady modest gains beat flashy inconsistency

summary.title: Reading and Exploiting Collisions
bullets: High rep: mirror opponents to guarantee first resolution
Low rep: never play a card you think someone else will choose
By last 2 days, you can predict opponents' plays with near certainty

summary.title: Voyage Pacing and Card Conservation
bullets: Leftover cards carry to next voyage — larger hand = more flexibility
Playing 4 of 6 in V1 gives you 8 cards entering V2 vs opponents' 6
Harder for opponents to predict your plays with a bigger hand

summary.title: Anchor Timing Optimization
bullets: Play anchor characters on mediocre loot days
Anchor ability provides value when loot itself is weak
Don't waste high-rank non-anchor characters on low-value loot

summary.title: Graveyard Synergies Across Voyages
bullets: Graveyard grows each voyage — 8+ characters by V3
Some characters score based on graveyard count/contents
Plan V1-V2 plays to set up V3 graveyard payoffs

summary.title: Multiplayer Threat Assessment
bullets: Monitor score track after each voyage — identify the leader
Target leader's strategy with counter-plays (steal maps, lower rep)
Balance advancing your position with keeping the leader in check

entries.definition: The first phase of each day. All players simultaneously choose one character card from their hand and place it face down. Once everyone is ready, all cards are revealed at the same time.
The second phase. Characters with a sun icon ability resolve their powers from lowest rank to highest rank. Low-numbered characters act first, giving them an early-action advantage.
The third phase. Starting with the highest-ranked character and going down, each player picks one loot token from the day's row. Higher rank gets first pick of the best loot.
The fourth phase. Characters with a moon icon ability resolve their powers from lowest rank to highest rank. Night abilities often depend on what loot was collected during Dusk.
After the Night phase, all played character cards move to their owner's ship tableau. The day is complete and the next day begins.
An ability that triggers during the Daytime phase, resolving from lowest rank to highest. Often aggressive or manipulative — stealing, discarding, or disrupting opponents.
An ability that triggers during loot collection at Dusk. May modify how you collect loot or trigger effects based on what loot is available.
An ability that triggers during the Night phase, resolving from lowest rank to highest. Often depends on what loot you collected or the current board state.

An ability that triggers at the end of a voyage, not during any specific day. Anchor characters reward you for what you accumulated during the entire voyage, such as bonus doubloons per treasure chest collected.
An ability that triggers when a character is discarded to the graveyard. Some characters also have ongoing effects that reference what is in your graveyard.
The number on a character card, ranging from one to forty. Rank determines the order of ability resolution (low to high for Daytime and Night) and loot selection (high to low for Dusk).
A track on the island board that breaks ties when two or more players play the same character. Higher reputation resolves first in all phases. Some abilities and loot tokens adjust reputation.
The victory point currency of Libertalia. Gained through loot tokens, character abilities, and end-of-voyage effects. The player with the most doubloons after three voyages wins.
One of three major rounds in the game. Each voyage consists of multiple days. After all days are complete, anchor abilities and loot effects resolve, then characters move to the graveyard.
A single round within a voyage consisting of four phases: Sunrise, Daytime, Dusk, and Night. The number of days per voyage depends on player count.
The core mechanism of Libertalia. All players choose their character card at the same time during Sunrise, then reveal simultaneously. This creates bluffing, prediction, and counterplay.
The area on your player board where played characters rest after each day. Characters on your ship may have ongoing effects and will have their anchor abilities resolved at voyage end.
The discard area on your player board. Characters move here from the ship at the end of each voyage. The graveyard is visible to all players and some characters reference its contents.
The first-player marker. Given to the starting player and may change hands during the game based on certain character abilities. Used to determine play order when needed.
The cloth bag containing all loot tokens. Tokens are drawn randomly from this bag at the start of each voyage to fill the island board's loot display.
The central game board showing the loot display for each day of the current voyage and the reputation track. All players interact with this shared board.
A loot token worth doubloons at the end of the voyage. One of the most straightforward and desirable loot types. Some anchor abilities grant bonus doubloons per chest collected.
A loot token that scores based on set collection. The more maps you collect across a voyage, the more each one is worth. Collecting three or four maps can yield a massive doubloon payoff.
A loot token that deducts doubloons at the end of the voyage. Generally avoided, but some characters have abilities that benefit from holding cursed relics or can force them onto opponents.
A loot token that provides combat-related bonuses. Sabers can be used with certain character abilities for additional effects during the voyage.
A loot token worth a fixed amount of doubloons at voyage end. Similar to treasure chests but typically with different values and sometimes different interaction with character abilities.
A loot token that provides reputation adjustments or other special effects at the end of the voyage. Amulets can shift the reputation track in your favor for future tiebreakers.

When two or more players play the same character card on the same day. Resolved by reputation: the player with higher reputation acts first in all phases. A key strategic consideration in card selection.

The mechanic where players keep leftover cards from previous voyages. Before each new voyage, six new cards are dealt, but unplayed cards carry over. Hands grow from roughly six cards in Voyage one to potentially ten or more by Voyage three.
The deduction element where players track which characters opponents have played. Since all players start with the same hand, played cards reveal information about what opponents still hold.
The sequence after the last day of a voyage: resolve anchor abilities on ship characters, resolve loot token effects, discard all loot tokens, and move ship characters to the graveyard.
The shared track where players record their doubloon totals. Updated throughout the game as characters gain or lose doubloons. The player with the highest total after three voyages wins.
A track on the island board where all players place their reputation tokens. Moving up grants tiebreaker priority. Some abilities and loot adjust position on this track.
The order used for Daytime and Night ability resolution. The lowest-ranked character resolves first, proceeding upward. This gives low-rank characters a first-mover advantage for abilities.
The order used for Dusk loot collection. The highest-ranked character picks first, proceeding downward. This gives high-rank characters first pick of the best available loot.
